1. A 62-year-old woman with a history of hypertension and diabetes presents with sudden-onset severe headache, nausea, and vomiting. She is found to have nuchal rigidity and photophobia. CT head is negative for hemorrhage. Lumbar puncture reveals xanthochromic CSF with elevated opening pressure and elevated RBCs. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Bacterial meningitis
Subarachnoid hemorrhage
Viral encephalitis
Hypertensive urgency
Migraine with aura
Subarachnoid hemorrhage
Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) classically presents as a sudden-onset "thunderclap" headache. A negative CT does not exclude SAH; lumbar puncture showing xanthochromia (yellow discoloration from RBC breakdown) and elevated RBCs confirms the diagnosis.
